The Africa News Edition
Complete Regional Briefing
News That Matters to Africa • "Overlooked and Misunderstood" ©
Monday, July 14, 2026
Video of the Day
Our Environmental Impact
A thought provoking look at the often unseen consequences of modern life and the footprint we leave on the planet
Watch on Instagram
From The Desk Of The Editor — Quote of the Day
"The people who built their Heaven on your land are telling you that yours…is in the sky"
Central Africa
Chad Security Crisis
Emergency Measures Declared After Renewed Displacement From Border Violence

Chad's government has announced additional emergency measures after renewed insecurity along its borders forced more civilians to flee their homes. Officials said security operations were being expanded to protect communities affected by armed violence and cross-border instability. Chad has faced increasing pressure from conflicts in neighbouring Sudan and wider instability across the Sahel, with hundreds of thousands of displaced people seeking refuge inside the country.

Humanitarian organisations warned that local authorities are struggling to provide adequate shelter, food and medical support. The government said it would strengthen coordination with aid agencies while maintaining security operations in affected areas.

Source: Reuters
Eastern Africa
DRC Ebola Treatment Trial
First Patients Enrolled in Record-Breaking Ebola Treatment Trial in DRC

There is no approved drug to help the medical teams scrabbling to save lives in the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo – but there are hopes that could change within months as the first patients are enrolled in a treatment trial. It is a record pace to set up and start this kind of research, scientists said, with patients enrolled just six weeks after the outbreak being declared a public health emergency of international concern by the World Health Organization (WHO) on 17 May.

The Partners treatment trial has opened with two drugs on its books – remdesivir, and MBP134. Patients will be randomly allocated to receive either drug, a combination of the two, or simply standard, supportive care. Both are given intravenously – MBP134 as a one-off infusion, and remdesivir as 10 days of intravenous therapy.

"These two drugs actually have been proven to work against the Bundibugyo virus in animal models," said Prof Laurens Liesenborghs of the Institute of Tropical Medicine, Antwerp, who is working on the trial in Ituri.

Source: The Guardian
DRC Vaccine Development
Oxford Begins First Human Trial of Bundibugyo Ebola Vaccine

The University of Oxford has launched the first human trial of a vaccine against Bundibugyo ebolavirus, seeking to accelerate efforts to combat an outbreak spreading in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda. The early-stage trial, known as BD-Ebov, will evaluate the safety and immune response of the ChAdOx1 BDBV vaccine in 50 healthy adults aged 18 to 55 in Oxford, the university said on Monday.

Recruitment has begun, with vaccinations expected to start in the coming weeks pending regulatory approval. The vaccine was developed by scientists at Oxford's Vaccine Group and Pandemic Sciences Institute using the same viral vector platform as the Oxford/AstraZeneca COVID-19 shot.

Serum Institute of India, which is partnering on the programme, said it manufactured and stockpiled about 620,000 doses of the vaccine candidate within two weeks and supplied 4,000 investigational doses for the early-stage study.

Source: Reuters
DRC Conflict Complication
AFC/M23 Rebels Build Parallel Ebola Response as Health Crisis Tests Congo's Divided Authority

The DRC's Ebola response has become entangled with the country's wider political and military crisis, as the AFC/M23 rebel movement has created its own health structures in areas under its control. According to Reuters, the group has operated a separate response system largely disconnected from authorities in Kinshasa, while receiving some support from neighbouring Rwanda, which denies backing the rebels.

The outbreak has provided AFC/M23 with an opportunity to demonstrate administrative control over territory seized during its rapid advance through North and South Kivu. Health officials and aid workers warn, however, that competing systems could complicate containment efforts if infections spread into frontline areas.

The situation highlights how Congo's conflict is increasingly shaping humanitarian operations.

Source: Reuters
DRC Misinformation Crisis
Ebola Misinformation Fuels Attacks on Health Workers as Outbreak Death Toll Rises

Health workers battling Congo's Ebola outbreak are facing a second challenge beyond the virus itself: misinformation. False claims surrounding the disease have contributed to attacks on medical facilities, intimidation of responders and resistance to public health measures, according to BBC reporting.

The outbreak, centred in eastern Congo, has infected more than 1,700 people and killed hundreds since emerging in May. Health officials say misinformation has complicated vaccination, contact tracing and treatment efforts, particularly in communities already affected by conflict and distrust of authorities.

Medical teams are working alongside local leaders to rebuild confidence, but insecurity and rumours continue to threaten the response.

Source: BBC
The Africa CDC issued an emergency epidemiological warning on Friday, 10 July 2026, revealing that the active Ebola virus outbreak in eastern DRC is expanding faster than local response capacities—the fastest-growing Ebola transmission event on record.
DRC Emergency Response
Africa CDC Deploys Emergency Teams as Ebola Outbreak Accelerates in DR Congo

The Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC) issued an emergency epidemiological warning on Friday, 10 July 2026, revealing that the active Ebola virus outbreak in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo is expanding faster than local response capacities. Public health officials characterized the situation as the fastest-growing Ebola transmission event on record.

The response is severely hampered by deep international aid cuts and widespread medical misinformation circulating within local communities. Furthermore, active conflict zones complicate medical logistics; security analysts warned that active rebel groups operating in the eastern provinces are actively exploiting the public health chaos and quarantine disruptions to expand their territorial control and consolidate political power.

Source: Africa Center for Strategic Studies (ACSS)
DRC Outbreak Expansion
Ebola Outbreak Spreads into Two More Provinces as Congo's Health Crisis Widens

The DRC's Ebola outbreak has expanded into two additional northeastern provinces, signalling a further geographical spread of the country's most serious health emergency in years. The National Institute of Public Health said the two provinces are now classified as epidemic zones after cases were detected beyond the outbreak's original centre in Ituri.

By July 11, Congo had recorded 1,926 confirmed cases and 702 deaths, according to official figures. Health officials remain concerned that the true scale of transmission may be higher because many infections have no clear epidemiological link.

The WHO has warned that population movement, insecurity and weak health access continue to complicate containment efforts.

Source: Reuters
Uganda Border Concerns
Uganda Linked to Congo Ebola Outbreak as Cross-Border Concerns Increase

Uganda's health authorities have continued monitoring Ebola risks after infections linked to the Democratic Republic of Congo's Bundibugyo strain crossed borders earlier in the outbreak. The WHO said Uganda had recorded confirmed cases connected to transmission originating in Congo, including infections among healthcare workers and contacts.

The situation has increased pressure on authorities along the border to strengthen surveillance, testing and community awareness campaigns. Uganda's experience with previous Ebola outbreaks has helped accelerate preparedness measures, but officials remain concerned about cross-border movement through busy transport and trade routes.

Source: WHO
Kenya Political Violence
Kenya's Goons: A World Of Political Violence and Desperation

Kenyan politicians are hiring armed thugs for as little as $4 per day, as poverty and ruthless political competition threaten to unleash uncontrollable violence ahead of elections next year. So-called "goons" are increasingly being hired, dozens or even hundreds at a time, to break up the rallies of political opponents and protesters, and attack civil rights groups — with police often turning a blind eye.

A recent study by Nairobi-based research firm Odipo Dev revealed a goons rate card, ranging from 500 shillings ($4) to work for a local councillor to as much as 1,000 shillings for members of parliament. Politicians and police insist they want to stamp out "goonism", but analysts say it is deeply entrenched on all sides.

During anti-government protests last year, journalists saw first-hand how hundreds of goons appeared to work directly alongside police to attack demonstrators, later looting and vandalising businesses in central Nairobi.

Kenya Political Violence: Citizens gather to voice concerns about rising political intimidation and the use of hired violence ahead of elections.
Source: AFP
Somalia Counter-Insurgency
Govt Forces Make Gains Against al-Shabaab but Security Challenges Remain

Somali government forces have reported new advances against al-Shabaab militants as military operations continue in central and southern parts of the country. Officials said recent operations targeted militant positions, supply routes and commanders involved in attacks against government forces and civilians.

Despite battlefield gains, security analysts warn that al-Shabaab remains capable of carrying out major attacks, particularly in urban areas where it maintains intelligence networks and operational reach. The government is under pressure to demonstrate that Somali forces can assume greater responsibility as international security support evolves.

The campaign remains one of Africa's longest-running counter-insurgency efforts, with poverty, political disputes and limited state capacity continuing to complicate efforts to establish lasting stability.

Source: BBC
South Sudan Humanitarian Crisis
Severe Drought Pushes South Sudan Toward Emergency Food Intervention

South Sudan is facing a worsening humanitarian emergency as severe drought conditions threaten communities already struggling with conflict, displacement and economic instability. Humanitarian agencies have activated emergency measures to protect vulnerable populations as rainfall shortages damage livelihoods and increase pressure on food supplies.

Millions of South Sudanese depend on agriculture and livestock, leaving rural communities particularly exposed when climate conditions deteriorate. Aid organisations warn that limited funding and difficult access could restrict the scale of assistance available.

The crisis comes as the country continues efforts to implement a fragile peace process while managing repeated humanitarian shocks. Relief agencies say early intervention will be critical to preventing a deeper food crisis.

Source: ReliefWeb
Sudan Crisis Coverage
Sudan War Crimes Trial
Govt's Court Sentences RSF Chief To Death Over War Crimes

A Sudanese court has sentenced the leader of the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo to death for war crimes, crimes against humanity and genocide over atrocities committed in the West Darfur region. The court in Port Sudan, a city under the army's rule, convicted Dagalo - known as Hemedti - in absentia, along with 15 other senior RSF members, who were given the same sentence.

The Sudan Founding Alliance, which includes the RSF, told the BBC it was "sham trial" that "does not even deserve a comment". The trial centred on alleged atrocities committed in the regional capital el-Geneina, including the June 2023 killing of the state governor, Khamis Abbakar.

This ruling is the first judicial conviction of the RSF's leadership since civil war broke out, however its impact remains unclear as the group continues to control large parts of western Sudan and its leaders remain beyond the reach of the army.

Sudan War Crimes Accountability: RSF Commander Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo (Hemedti) faces multiple judicial proceedings for alleged atrocities in Darfur.
Source: BBC
ICC Investigation
ICC Says Breakthrough Evidence Links RSF Commanders to Darfur War Crimes

The International Criminal Court says investigators have made a significant breakthrough in gathering evidence linking senior Rapid Support Forces commanders to alleged war crimes in Sudan's Darfur region. ICC deputy prosecutor Nazhat Shameem Khan told the BBC that investigators had gathered material relating to atrocities committed during the conflict, including killings and sexual violence.

The investigation focuses on abuses reported during the RSF's campaign in areas including El Geneina and El Fasher, where international observers have warned of mass atrocities. The RSF has denied targeting civilians.

The development marks one of the most significant international accountability efforts since Sudan's war began, as millions remain displaced and diplomatic efforts to end the conflict continue.

Source: BBC
European Parliament Resolution
European Parliament Passes Resolution Demanding Terrorist Designation for Sudan's RSF

The European Parliament adopted a major resolution on Thursday, 9 July 2026, formally calling on the European Union to designate Sudan's Rapid Support Forces (RSF) as a terrorist organization. The legislative measure specifically cited the paramilitary group's prolonged military siege of El-Obeid and widespread, documented attacks targeting civilian populations.

In addition to the terrorist listing, the European lawmakers demanded strict financial and travel sanctions against individual RSF commanders held responsible for human rights violations in Darfur and Kordofan. The resolution also took aim at the paramilitary group's transnational supply lines, demanding immediate asset freezes and targeted sanctions against a United Arab Emirates-based front company allegedly facilitating logistics and weapons procurement for the RSF.

Source: ACSS
Sudan Peace Initiative
Sudan's Army Sets Conditions for Joining US-Backed Peace Initiative

Sudan's army has signalled conditional support for a United States-backed peace initiative aimed at ending the country's devastating civil war, but said any agreement must guarantee the withdrawal of the Rapid Support Forces from areas they currently control. The Sudanese Armed Forces said negotiations could only succeed if the RSF abandons occupied cities and returns control of state institutions to the government.

The RSF has previously rejected demands it views as surrender conditions and has called for a political settlement involving all sides. The competing positions underline the deep divisions blocking diplomatic efforts more than two years after fighting erupted between the two forces.

The conflict has displaced millions and created one of the world's largest humanitarian emergencies.

Source: Reuters
Sudan Cholera Crisis
Cholera Outbreak Worsens as Sudan's War Disrupts Health System

Sudan's cholera crisis has intensified as heavy rains, displacement and the collapse of medical infrastructure combine to accelerate the spread of the disease. Health authorities reported a sharp increase in suspected cases, with humanitarian agencies warning that contaminated water supplies and overcrowded displacement sites are creating ideal conditions for transmission.

The outbreak has affected communities already weakened by hunger and prolonged conflict, particularly in areas where hospitals and vaccination programmes have been disrupted. Aid organisations say access restrictions and insecurity are preventing medical teams from reaching some of the worst-affected areas.

The latest health emergency adds another layer to Sudan's humanitarian catastrophe, where millions are already facing severe food shortages and displacement.

Source: Reuters
Tanzania Dissent Crackdown
130 People Arrested as Government Cracks Down on Dissent

Tanzanian police have arrested 130 people for "inciting criminal acts", they said on Thursday, as part of efforts to contain dissent after a ban on all political rallies. Police said in a statement that they were working to track down other suspects.

Before the ban on rallies, announced by the interior minister on June 26, protesters had planned to gather on the July 7 public holiday to call for democratic reforms and justice for people killed in election violence last year. Heavy security deployments prevented the protests from happening.

A government-appointed commission of inquiry said in April that at least 518 people were killed in the election violence, which was driven by the exclusion of leading opposition candidates. Tanzanian authorities have denied allegations by human rights groups that the security services used excessive force during the election unrest.

Source: Reuters
North Africa
Egypt Maritime Policy
Egypt Refuses Entry to LGBTQ+ Cruise Ship Following Similar Rejection by Turkey

An international cruise liner carrying LGBTQ+ passengers was officially denied permission to dock at an Egyptian port on Thursday, 9 July 2026. The maritime refusal by Egyptian port authorities occurred just days after the vessel was turned away from its planned stop in Turkey under similar state directives.

Egyptian border control and maritime transit officials cited local administrative codes to justify the block, preventing hundreds of international tourists from disembarking. Human rights organizations monitored the standoff, noting that the incident underscores expanding regional restrictions on civil liberties and the targeting of non-traditional lifestyle tourism within the Mediterranean basin, despite the economic reliance of local coastal commercial hubs on international cruise traffic.

Source: The Guardian
Libya Military Coordination
Rival Military Chiefs Meet in Sirte to Revive Army Unification Drive

Rival military commanders from eastern and western Libya resumed talks on Sunday aimed at reunifying the country's armed forces, as efforts to overcome years of institutional division gathered fresh momentum despite the absence of any announced breakthrough. The meeting, held in the central coastal city of Sirte, brought together senior officers from both sides of Libya's military divide, alongside members of the Joint Military Commission (5+5), the Joint Security and Military Committee (3+3) and representatives from the United Nations Support Mission in Libya.

The gathering is the latest step in a long-running process launched after the 2020 UN-brokered ceasefire that ended major fighting between Libya's rival factions but failed to reunify the country's fractured security institutions. No details were released on the substance of the discussions, and participants announced no concrete agreements, reflecting the sensitivity of one of Libya's most politically contentious issues.

For more than a decade, Libya has maintained two parallel military structures mirroring its broader political split.

Source: The Arab Weekly
Morocco 2030 World Cup
Morocco Pivots Commercial Focus to 2030 World Cup After National Team Elimination

Following the national team's exit from the ongoing FIFA World Cup tournament, the Moroccan government on Monday, 13 July 2026, officially shifted its state focus entirely toward long-term infrastructure and logistical planning for the 2030 World Cup. Morocco is scheduled to co-host the 2030 global tournament alongside Spain and Portugal.

Despite immediate public disappointment over the team's elimination during the knockout stages, the Ministry of Sports and municipal planning agencies in Rabat, Casablanca, and Marrakech announced that construction timelines for massive stadium expansions, high-speed rail lines, and hospitality infrastructure will accelerate ahead of the 2030 deadline to maximize tourism returns.

Source: NTV Afrika
Southern Africa
Mozambique Security Operations
Security Operations Intensify as Cabo Delgado Insurgency Continues

Mozambican security forces have intensified operations against armed groups in Cabo Delgado province as authorities attempt to restore stability in an area critical to the country's natural gas ambitions. Government officials said recent operations targeted militant positions and sought to reopen transport routes affected by insecurity.

The insurgency has displaced hundreds of thousands of residents and repeatedly delayed major energy investments. Regional forces from Southern Africa have supported Mozambique's counter-insurgency campaign, although the security situation remains unpredictable.

Humanitarian organisations warned that renewed fighting could worsen displacement and increase pressure on already vulnerable communities. The government says restoring security in Cabo Delgado remains essential to reviving economic development in the north.

Source: Nation
South Africa Immigration Crisis
South Africa Evacuates 53,000 Foreigners Amid Massive Anti-Immigration Campaign

The government of South Africa confirmed on Sunday, 12 July 2026, that its immigration and security agencies have formally deported over 53,000 foreign nationals as part of a sweeping, state-wide anti-migration crackdown. Driven by prolonged public demonstrations blaming undocumented migrants for high domestic unemployment, rising crime indexes, and the strain on public services, the situation escalated into targeted house-to-house searches by localized protest groups.

In response to safety threats, the governments of Nigeria, Ghana, Uganda, and Malawi initiated emergency repatriation operations. The Nigerian government processed an additional 266 citizens through transit centers on Friday, bringing total evacuation figures to historic highs as the diplomatic crisis deepens.

South Africa Xenophobic Crisis: Mass demonstrations reflect tensions over migration and economic hardship, resulting in widespread displacement and international concern.
Source: BBC
South Africa Humanitarian Response
MSF Launches Emergency Response as Xenophobic Attacks Intensify in South Africa

The surge in anti-migrant violence and intimidation across South Africa has displaced tens of thousands of people and disrupted access to essential healthcare for many affected communities, prompting Doctors Without Borders (MSF) to launch an emergency medical humanitarian response.

The violence has resulted in at least four deaths, numerous injuries and the destruction of homes. The organisation said its teams have been responding to urgent health, protection and dignity needs in Gauteng, KwaZulu-Natal, the Western Cape, and at the South Africa–Zimbabwe border towns of Musina and Beitbridge.

"MSF is particularly concerned about continuity of care for people living with chronic diseases such as diabetes, hypertension, mental health, HIV and TB where lack of treatment or medication interruptions can lead to serious health complications", says MSF nurse Phumla Tsotetsi.

The tensions have forced tens of thousands of people, many from Malawi, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Nigeria and Ghana, to flee their homes due to fears for their safety and threats to their livelihoods.

Source: Eastleigh Voice
Regional leaders warn that violence against migrants risks damaging South Africa's reputation as a continental leader and undermining its long-standing advocacy for human rights.
South Africa Regional Relations
Anti-Migrant Protests Deepen Diplomatic Tensions Across Southern Africa

South Africa faced growing regional criticism after renewed anti-migrant protests triggered diplomatic concern among neighbouring governments. Several African states called on Pretoria to protect foreign nationals following reports of attacks targeting migrants and foreign-owned businesses. The South African government said police had been deployed to affected areas and insisted that criminal acts would be investigated.

The protests have intensified debate over unemployment, illegal migration and public frustration with economic hardship. Regional leaders warned that violence against migrants risks damaging South Africa's reputation as a continental leader and undermining its long-standing advocacy for human rights. The issue has become a major diplomatic challenge for President Cyril Ramaphosa's administration.

Source: Reuters
South Africa Political Debate
South Africa's Opposition Challenges Government Over Migrant Crisis Response

South Africa's political opposition has intensified criticism of the government's handling of rising anti-migrant tensions, accusing authorities of failing to address the causes of growing hostility toward foreign nationals. Opposition leaders argue that unemployment, weak border management and poor enforcement of immigration laws have been allowed to fuel resentment and create conditions for violence.

The government has rejected claims that it has ignored the issue, saying law enforcement agencies are acting against criminal activity while protecting communities. The debate has renewed concerns about South Africa's image across Africa, particularly as neighbouring governments call for stronger protection of their citizens living and working in the country.

Source: BBC
Zambia Elections
Election Campaign Enters Tense Phase Ahead of August Vote

Zambia's political landscape has entered a more competitive phase as parties prepare for the August general election, with opposition groups accusing President Hakainde Hichilema's government of restricting political space. The government has rejected the accusations, saying authorities are committed to maintaining stability and allowing peaceful campaigning.

The election is expected to focus heavily on economic issues, including the cost of living, debt management and job creation. Hichilema came to power in 2021 promising economic recovery and improved relations with international investors, but voters continue to express frustration over persistent financial pressures.

Election observers say the credibility of the vote will depend on equal treatment of candidates and confidence in the electoral process.

Source: IOL
Zimbabwe Constitutional Changes
Opposition Rejects Constitutional Changes Extending Presidential Timeline

Zimbabwe's opposition parties have intensified their challenge against constitutional amendments that critics say could extend President Emmerson Mnangagwa's political influence beyond existing limits. The government argues that the changes are aimed at improving governance and reducing the cost of elections, but opposition leaders say they undermine democratic safeguards.

Civil society groups have also questioned whether the amendments received sufficient public consultation. The dispute has triggered renewed debate about constitutional protections, electoral competition and the future direction of Zimbabwe's political system.

The country has faced years of economic difficulties and international criticism over governance issues, making the constitutional debate a major test of public confidence in state institutions.

Source: Associated Press
West Africa
Burkina Faso Mining Policy
Govt Takes Full State Control of $68 Million Gold Mine, Marks Shift Away from Foreign Miners

Burkina Faso's military-led government has approved, at the Council of Ministers' weekly cabinet meeting, a mining permit for gold development to its state-owned SOPAMIB Bouboulou S.A. The move represents a decisive break from decades of reliance on Western and private corporations to extract the country's mineral wealth.

Located in the north-central Yako commune, the Bouboulou mine contains an estimated 10.77 million tonnes of ore. It is expected to generate nearly 39 billion CFA francs (approximately $68.4 million) in direct government revenue through fees and taxes, excluding dividends. The mine is also expected to create and sustain more than 1,200 direct and indirect jobs.

Foreign junior mining firms previously held the exploration permits, retaining full operational control over project development while the Burkinabè state was relegated to collecting minor royalties and holding non-controlling equity stakes. Since assuming leadership, Captain Ibrahim Traoré has focused on rewriting resource management laws to keep resource wealth within local borders.

Burkina Faso Resource Sovereignty: Military leadership oversees gold recovery from the Bouboulou mine, part of government efforts to retain control of natural resources.
Source: West Africa Weekly
Burkina Faso Security Expansion
Burkina Faso Expands Civilian Mobilization as Jihadist Attacks Continue

Burkina Faso's military government has expanded its reliance on civilian defence groups as it struggles to contain escalating attacks by armed Islamist groups. Authorities announced further mobilization measures aimed at supporting the national army in areas where militants continue to challenge state control.

The move reflects the junta's broader strategy of reducing dependence on foreign military partners while building domestic security capacity. Human rights organisations have repeatedly warned that the use of armed civilian auxiliaries carries risks of abuses against local communities.

The security crisis has displaced millions of people and weakened government authority across large parts of the country. Officials insist the expanded mobilization is necessary to restore national security.

Source: Associated Press
Ghana Extradition
Social-Media Influencer Extradited to US Over Fraud

The government of Ghana finalized the extradition of prominent Ghanaian socialite Abu Trica to the United States on Thursday, 9 July 2026. Trica was taken into custody by federal law enforcement agents upon landing on American soil, following a months-long legal battle in Ghanaian courts.

The United States Department of Justice has indicted Trica on multiple federal counts, including wire fraud, bank fraud, and money laundering, stemming from an alleged international cyber-fraud scheme that siphoned more than $8 million from American citizens and corporate entities.

Ghana's Ministry of the Interior stated that the smooth extradition underscores deep, ongoing bilateral security cooperation between Accra and Washington in dismantling transnational syndicates.

Source: BBC
Mali Insurgency
Insurgents Withdraw from Strategic Town after Days of Fighting

A Tuareg-led separatist group has begun withdrawing from Mali's strategic northern town of Anefis after armed forces including members of Russia's paramilitary Africa Corps pushed them out, a rebel spokesperson and the army said on Friday.

The rebel group, the Azawad Liberation Front (FLA), and Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal Muslimin (JNIM), last weekend staged attacks on army positions across Mali, including Anefis. The strategic town links Gao, the north's biggest city, to the town of Kidal which was seized by FLA fighters earlier this year.

Mohamed Elmaouloud Ramadane, a spokesperson for the FLA, told Reuters the group's fighters on Thursday had decided to withdraw after a week of clashes with military convoys coming from Gao. Mali's armed forces said in a statement on Friday they were continuing joint operations with Africa Corps across the country.

Source: Reuters
Sahel Regional Coordination
Sahel Alliance Deepens Military Coordination as Regional Tensions Rise

Mali, Burkina Faso and Niger have continued strengthening their joint security arrangements under the Alliance of Sahel States as the three military-led governments seek greater independence from traditional international partners. Defence officials from the three countries say closer cooperation is necessary to confront expanding militant threats across the region.

The alliance has increasingly replaced older regional frameworks after the countries withdrew from the Economic Community of West African States. Critics argue that the new security structure lacks the resources and coordination needed to reverse worsening instability, while supporters say it reflects a demand for African-led solutions.

The developments come as jihadist groups continue to operate across large parts of the central Sahel.

Source: Associated Press
Nigeria Counter-Banditry
Nigerian Military Eliminates 300 Armed Bandits in Zamfara State Counter-Offensive

The Nigerian Army executed a massive anti-banditry operation in the northwestern state of Zamfara, neutralizing over 300 armed bandits, defense officials confirmed on Saturday, 11 July 2026. The intensive military campaign combined targeted airstrikes by the Nigerian Air Force with synchronized ground assaults by infantry brigades.

The operation successfully dismantled multiple fortified insurgent camps located deep within rural border areas. The targeted criminal syndicates had long terrorized local farming communities, orchestrating mass kidnappings for ransom, cattle rustling, and illegal gold mining.

Security forces recovered an extensive cache of sophisticated weaponry, including assault rifles and rocket-propelled grenades, while establishing new forward operating bases to prevent insurgent regrouping.

Source: The Guardian
Nigeria Rescue Operation
Nigerian Security Forces Rescue 39 Abducted Schoolchildren in Oyo State Operation

Military and police tactical units successfully rescued 39 schoolchildren and five teachers in Oyo State on Sunday, 12 July 2026. The students and educators had been held in captivity by armed criminal gangs for nearly two months following their mass abduction from an educational facility.

State security officials confirmed that a well-coordinated kinetic raid penetrated the kidnappers' forest hideout, neutralizing armed resistance without civilian casualties. Authorities confirmed that eight suspected ringleaders and accomplices were arrested during the clearing operation and are currently undergoing interrogation.

The rescued victims were immediately transported to a military medical facility for physical evaluation and psychological trauma counseling before reunification with family members.

Source: Al Jazeera
Nigeria Defence Innovation
Can Nigeria's Drone Industry Deliver Africa's Defence Sovereignty

For decades, African militaries have turned abroad for critical defence technologies, leaving the continent largely positioned as a buyer rather than a producer. An Abuja-based start-up is attempting to change that equation.

Terra Industries, founded in 2024 by Nathan Nwachuku and Maxwell Maduka, both in their early twenties, designs and manufactures drones, autonomous surveillance towers and unmanned ground vehicles from facilities in Abuja and Accra. Unlike companies that primarily assemble imported components, Terra says it develops its own software, airframes, propellers and lithium-ion battery packs, with more than 70 percent of its inputs sourced locally.

The interest in companies like Terra comes as drones become increasingly central to conflicts across Africa. In the Sahel, inexpensive commercial drones have moved from surveillance tools to weapons used on the battlefield, creating new challenges for militaries that often lack effective counter-drone capabilities.

Source: Al Jazeera
Senegal Constitutional Law
Senegal's Top Court Blocks Constitutional Reform Plan

Senegal's Constitutional Council has rejected a proposed reform package that had been promoted by the government as part of efforts to reshape state institutions. The decision represents a setback for President Bassirou Diomaye Faye's administration, which came to power promising major political and governance reforms.

The court ruled that elements of the proposal conflicted with constitutional provisions, forcing the government to reconsider its approach. Supporters of the reforms argued they were necessary to modernise institutions and improve accountability, while critics had warned that some changes could weaken existing safeguards.

The ruling highlights the continuing balance between the reform ambitions of Senegal's new leadership and the authority of established constitutional institutions.

Senegal Constitutional Debate: Government representatives at the Palace of the Republic address proposed constitutional amendments that faced judicial rejection.
Source: ACSS
Continental & Thematic Coverage
Africa Global Finance
African Leaders Push for Stronger Role in Global Financial System Reform

African leaders are increasing pressure for changes to international financial institutions, arguing that current structures leave developing economies disadvantaged in accessing affordable financing. Governments across the continent say high borrowing costs, debt pressures and limited access to climate finance are restricting development efforts.

The push includes calls for greater African representation in institutions such as the International Monetary Fund and World Bank, alongside reforms to lending systems. Supporters argue that Africa's economic potential requires a financial architecture better suited to emerging economies.

Critics caution that domestic governance reforms and stronger financial management remain essential if additional resources are to translate into sustainable growth.

Source: Financial Times
Africa Diplomatic Voice
African Union Presses for Stronger Continental Voice as Global Crises Reshape Diplomacy

The African Union is intensifying calls for a greater African role in global decision-making as conflicts, economic pressures and climate challenges reshape international diplomacy. AU officials argue that Africa's growing strategic importance in energy, minerals, trade and security requires reforms to global institutions that better reflect the continent's interests.

The push comes as African governments seek greater influence in discussions on debt reform, peacekeeping and climate financing. While the continent has secured a permanent seat at the G20, leaders say representation alone is insufficient without meaningful participation in decisions affecting Africa's future.

Diplomats acknowledge that internal divisions among member states remain a challenge as the AU seeks to translate collective ambition into political influence.

African Union Diplomacy: Leaders convene at the African Union headquarters to discuss continental priorities and global engagement.
Source: AJ
Africa 2026 World Cup
Africa Reflects on Strongest World Cup Campaign as Football Power Balance Shifts

Africa's 2026 World Cup campaign has been widely viewed as a breakthrough moment despite no African team reaching the semi-finals. Nine of the continent's ten representatives advanced beyond the group stage, marking one of Africa's strongest collective performances at a global tournament.

Morocco again led the way by reaching the quarter-finals, while Cape Verde captured international attention with a dramatic run that ended against Argentina. Egypt secured its first World Cup victory and pushed Argentina in a closely contested knockout match. Algeria, Ghana and South Africa also reached the later stages, reinforcing the growing competitiveness of African football.

The tournament has strengthened expectations ahead of future global competitions, including Africa's upcoming hosting roles.

Source: ESPN
Humanitarian Innovation
Humanitarians Look to Put the AI in Aid

At the 'AI for Good' summit in Geneva this week, a handful of humanitarian-focused displays emphasised the technology's positive potential. Parked in one corner of a vast hall at the Palexpo conference centre was a giant white SHERP vehicle, resembling a hulking Martian rover, decked out with cameras and sensors and a drone landing-pad on the roof. Made in Ukraine, SHERPs are amphibious vehicles that can float on water, drive through swamps and flooded rivers with their giant wheels, and climb over obstacles up to one metre (3.3 feet) high.

The UN's World Food Programme (WFP) is preparing to begin field-testing a version of the AI-enabled truck that can be steered remotely through the most dangerous and difficult terrain to reach people in need. WFP already has drivers using SHERPs to deliver aid in Sudan, South Sudan and Uganda. But after numerous heartbreaking losses of drivers, it tasked the German Aerospace Center (DLR) to help equip the vehicles with AI and other technologies, making it possible to control them remotely through particularly dangerous terrain.

Source: RFI
The Africa News Edition
News That Matters to Africa • "Overlooked and Misunderstood" ©
